assumptions: The question concerns consolidated financial statements, not individual or separate financial statements.
The intragroup dividend has already been declared and is recognised as an intercompany receivable/payable.
The dividend is denominated in a currency that may differ from one or both counterparties' functional currencies.
No facts are provided that the dividend forms part of a net investment hedge designation.

approaches.label: General prohibition for intragroup items in consolidation
approaches.reasoning: In consolidated financial statements, hedged items generally must be with parties external to the reporting entity. Intragroup transactions are excluded unless a specific exception applies.
approaches.practical_implication: An intragroup dividend receivable is not hedge-eligible in consolidation by default.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.5 says only external-party items qualify in consolidation, with limited exceptions for intragroup items.

approaches.label: Exception for foreign currency risk on an intragroup monetary item
approaches.reasoning: IFRS 9 permits foreign currency risk on an intragroup monetary item in consolidated financial statements if the related FX gains or losses are not fully eliminated on consolidation. A recognised intragroup dividend receivable/payable can be analysed through that receivable/payable exception.
conditions: The dividend is a recognised intragroup monetary receivable/payable
The counterparties have different functional currencies
The foreign exchange gains or losses are not fully eliminated on consolidation
The hedge is formally designated and documented at inception
The hedge meets IFRS 9 effectiveness requirements
approaches.practical_implication: Hedge documentation may be possible, but only for the residual consolidated FX exposure that survives elimination.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.6 allows foreign currency risk on intragroup monetary items if the FX effects are not fully eliminated on consolidation.
Paragraph 6.4.1 requires formal designation, documentation, an economic relationship, non-dominant credit risk and an appropriate hedge ratio.

approaches.label: Forecast intragroup transaction cash flow hedge route
approaches.reasoning: This route applies only to highly probable forecast intragroup transactions whose FX risk affects consolidated profit or loss. A dividend already recognised as receivable is not a forecast transaction.
approaches.practical_implication: The forecast-transaction exception should not be used once the dividend has been booked as an intercompany receivable.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.6 permits certain highly probable forecast intragroup transactions, and B6.3.5-B6.3.6 limit this to exposures affecting consolidated profit or loss.

approaches.label: Net investment hedge model
approaches.reasoning: The net investment hedge model applies to foreign currency risk on a net investment in a foreign operation, with the hedged item being an amount of net assets. An intragroup dividend receivable is a different exposure from the net investment itself.
approaches.practical_implication: Do not rely on the net investment hedge model to support hedge documentation for an intragroup dividend receivable.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.5.2 identifies a hedge of a net investment in a foreign operation as a separate hedge type.
IFRIC 16 says the hedged item in a net investment hedge is an amount of net assets of the foreign operation, not an intercompany receivable.
